name: ds-search-bar
description: Composite search control: an internal text field and a trigger button, rendered as the component's own shadow parts. Fires ds-search on button press or Enter.

events.name: ds-search
events.description: Fired on Enter in the input or on trigger-button press.

constraints.rationale: No primitive references, no raw values.

The trigger's action-bg/action-fg pairing must clear AA in both contexts (already a declared ds.contrastPair); the host always carries an accessible name via aria-label mirroring `placeholder`; never a positive tabindex.
Adaptive class: API drift is structural drift.

provenance.author: ai
provenance.justification: Design decision: the internal input and trigger are rendered as this component's OWN shadow parts (plain <input>/<button> exposed via ::part()) rather than as nested real <ds-text-input> and <ds-button> custom-element instances, even though composition.allowedChildren documents ds-text-input and ds-button as the conceptually equivalent building blocks. Rejected alternative: literally instantiating <ds-text-input> and <ds-button> inside the shadow root. Rejected for two reasons: first, ds-button.composition.allowedParents (locked, human-authored, not editable by this AI-authored definition) names ds-form-field and ds-search-bar, and ds-search-bar.composition.allowedChildren was required to name ds-button back -- a literal nested-instance implementation would additionally need a real, registered ds-button INSIDE ds-search-bar's own registration-time render check, but ds-button itself cannot register until ds-search-bar already exists (its allowedParents check is closed-world too), which is an unbreakable circular registration dependency given both lists are fixed by requirement. Rendering plain shadow parts instead of nested custom elements breaks that cycle: ds-search-bar declares no 'composition' constraint and no 'composition' data block at all: it has no slots, so nothing can ever be slotted into it, and per the project's core rule an unenforced allowedChildren list is a wish, not a rule -- deleted rather than kept as documentation. The registry can then resolve ds-button's allowedParents for real once both molecules exist. Second-order decision: Enter fires ds-search from the input (not only button click), matching how people actually use search boxes.
